title: Bigger, Cheaper, Longer
subtitle: MoE, Long Context, and the New Architectures Racing Past Attention
theme: The efficiency frontier — how models got cheaper to run, longer in memory, and faster than the math said they should be.
learningObjectives: Explain Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and why a sparse model can be huge yet cheap to run
Describe the long-context problem and the techniques that broke the quadratic wall (FlashAttention, rotary embeddings, ring/streaming attention)
Understand why context windows leapt from 2K tokens to millions, and what 'effective' context really means
Compare Transformers with the challengers — Mamba, state-space models, and linear attention
Reason about the cost/quality/latency trade-offs an engineer actually faces in June 2026
Read a model spec sheet and tell whether 'parameters' means what your wallet thinks it means
storyHook: In 2017, a Transformer that read a single page of text was a marvel. By 2026, a model can ingest an entire codebase, a feature-length screenplay, and your last year of email in one breath — and it costs less per token than it did to read that single page. This is not magic. It is three quiet revolutions stacked on top of each other: sparsity, smarter attention, and a new family of architectures that asked a heretical question — what if attention isn't all you need?

slides.title: The Three Walls
slides.body:
## Where we are in the story

For the first fifteen days we watched models get *smarter*. Today we watch them get *practical*.

By 2022, the field hit three walls at once:

1. **The cost wall** — a dense model uses *every* parameter for *every* token. A 175B model thinks with all 175 billion neurons just to finish the sentence 'the cat sat on the ___'. Wasteful.
2. **The memory wall** — attention compares every token to every other token. Double the input, *quadruple* the compute. Long documents became financially impossible.
3. **The architecture wall** — the Transformer is brilliant but it is not sacred. Its quadratic cost is baked into its DNA.

Today is the story of how the field climbed all three.

> **The big idea:** The frontier of 2024-2026 was not just *bigger brains* — it was *cheaper brains, longer memory, and new blueprints*.
slides.speakerNotes: Open by asking the class: 'If I gave you a 500-page contract and asked one question, would you re-read every page for every word of your answer?' That waste is exactly what dense models do. Set up the three walls as the spine of the whole day.
slides.marketConnection: Every frontier model shipping in June 2026 — from the GPT, Gemini, Claude and Llama families to the Chinese open-weight wave — uses at least one of today's three tricks. None are optional anymore.

slides.title: Mixture-of-Experts: A Hospital, Not a Genius
slides.body:
## The core analogy

Imagine two hospitals.

**Hospital A (dense model):** every patient is seen by *all 500 doctors at once*. Brilliant care, absurd cost.

**Hospital B (MoE):** a sharp **receptionist (the router)** glances at each patient and sends them to the *2 most relevant specialists* out of 500. Same depth of expertise available, a fraction of the cost per visit.

That receptionist is the whole trick.

## What MoE actually is

In a Mixture-of-Experts layer, the usual feed-forward block is replaced by **many parallel expert networks** plus a **gating/router network** that picks which few to activate per token.

```
            token
              |
         [ ROUTER ]   <- tiny network, scores all experts
          /  |  \
     pick top-k (e.g. 2 of 64)
        /        \
   [Expert 12]  [Expert 47]   <- only these run
        \        /
         weighted sum
              |
           output
```

## The magic number: total vs. active parameters

This is the single most misread stat in the industry.

| Term | Meaning | Drives... |
|------|---------|-----------|
| **Total parameters** | All experts combined | Memory / VRAM to *hold* the model |
| **Active parameters** | The few that fire per token | Compute / *cost* per token |

A model can be **400B total but only 17B active**. It has the *knowledge capacity* of a giant and the *running cost* of a midsize model. That is the entire business case for MoE.
slides.speakerNotes: Hammer the total-vs-active distinction — it is the takeaway that makes learners look smart in a meeting. Ask: 'If a vendor says 1 trillion parameters, what's the FIRST question you ask?' Answer: how many are ACTIVE per token? That's what you pay for.
slides.marketConnection: By June 2026, MoE is the default for frontier-scale models. Mixtral lit the open-source fuse, DeepSeek's MoE models stunned the market on cost, and the largest closed models are widely understood to be sparse. 'Active parameters' is now a standard line on every serious spec sheet.

slides.title: Why MoE Is Hard (The Receptionist Has Problems)
slides.body:
## Sparsity is not free

MoE sounds like a free lunch. It isn't. The engineering pain is real and worth understanding.

### 1. Load balancing — the popular-doctor problem
If the router falls in love with Expert 12 and sends it 90% of traffic, you've effectively got a small dense model and 63 idle experts gathering dust. Training uses an **auxiliary load-balancing loss** to nudge the router toward spreading work evenly.

### 2. The memory tax
You still have to *store* all 400B parameters even though only 17B run. MoE saves **compute**, not **memory**. You need the VRAM of a giant to host it — which is why MoE shines in datacenters and struggles on your laptop.

### 3. Communication overhead
In a multi-GPU cluster, experts live on different chips. Routing a token to the right expert means shuffling data across the network — the dreaded **all-to-all** communication. At scale this can dominate the cost.

### 4. Training instability
Routers can collapse, oscillate, or make discrete decisions that gradients hate. MoE training is famously finicky.

> **Mental model:** MoE trades a *simple, expensive* problem (dense compute) for a *cheap, complicated* one (routing logistics). The field decided the complexity is worth it — and tooling caught up.
slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize the memory tax: many students assume MoE makes models small. It makes them CHEAP TO RUN, not SMALL TO STORE. This is why MoE didn't suddenly make laptops run GPT-4-class models.
slides.marketConnection: The 2025-2026 GPU shortage made MoE's compute savings extremely attractive to cloud providers — but the memory tax kept the very largest sparse models locked inside well-funded datacenters, reinforcing the cloud-vs-edge divide we'll explore on Day 18.

slides.title: The Quadratic Curse
slides.body:
## Why long documents used to be impossible

Self-attention's superpower is that every token sees every other token. Its curse is the *cost* of that: for a sequence of length **N**, attention does roughly **N x N** comparisons.

```
Sequence length   Comparisons (N^2)   Feel
-------------------------------------------------
   1,000          1,000,000           fine
  10,000        100,000,000           expensive
 100,000     10,000,000,000           brutal
 1,000,000  1,000,000,000,000         historically impossible
```

Double the input and the cost **quadruples**. This is the **quadratic curse**, and for years it capped context windows at a few thousand tokens. A model literally could not afford to read a long PDF.

## Two ways to fight back

The field attacked from two directions:

- **Make the *same* attention cheaper to compute** — without changing the math, change how the hardware runs it. (FlashAttention)
- **Change the math** so cost grows *linearly* instead of quadratically. (Linear attention, Mamba — later slides)

Both matter. The first kept Transformers alive at long context; the second is trying to replace them.
slides.speakerNotes: Draw the N^2 table on the board live and let the trillion-comparison number land. Ask: 'Why couldn't 2019 models read a whole book?' — the answer is sitting in that bottom row.
slides.marketConnection: The race to long context became a headline marketing battle in 2024-2026: every major lab advertised its window size, with Gemini's million-plus-token context becoming a signature differentiator.

slides.title: FlashAttention: Same Math, Smarter Memory
slides.body:
## The insight nobody expected

FlashAttention (Tri Dao and colleagues, 2022, refined through v2 and v3) did something almost philosophical: it sped up attention **without changing a single number in the result**. Same output, dramatically faster and lighter.

How? By respecting how GPUs *actually* work.

## The kitchen analogy

A GPU has two kinds of memory:
- **HBM** — huge but slow. The walk-in freezer at the back of the kitchen.
- **SRAM** — tiny but lightning-fast. The cutting board right in front of the chef.

Naive attention writes the giant N x N attention matrix to the slow freezer, then reads it back — a parade of slow trips to the back room. FlashAttention **never builds the full matrix**. It streams the computation in small tiles that fit on the fast cutting board, fusing the steps so data rarely touches slow memory.

```
Naive:   compute huge matrix -> write to slow HBM -> read back -> softmax -> ...
Flash:   tile by tile, stay in fast SRAM, fuse everything, write once
```

The result: attention became **memory-efficient and much faster**, especially at long sequence lengths. This is *the* unlock that made long-context Transformers economically viable.

> **The lesson for engineers:** sometimes the biggest speedup isn't a better algorithm — it's an algorithm that finally respects the hardware it runs on.
slides.speakerNotes: This is a great 'aha' slide. Stress that FlashAttention is exact, not approximate — same answer, less waste. Tie it to a life lesson: knowing your tools' physical limits beats raw cleverness.
slides.marketConnection: FlashAttention is now baked into nearly every serious training and inference stack in 2026. It is invisible infrastructure — the kind of optimization that quietly saves the industry billions in GPU hours.

slides.title: Rotary Embeddings: Teaching Position by Rotation
slides.body:
## A model has no sense of order — unless you give it one

Attention, by itself, is **order-blind**. Scramble the words and the raw mechanism barely notices. We have to *inject* a sense of position. Early Transformers added fixed or learned 'position vectors' — but those didn't stretch gracefully to lengths the model never saw in training.

## RoPE — Rotary Position Embedding

RoPE (Su et al.) had an elegant idea: instead of *adding* a position signal, **rotate** each token's query and key vectors by an angle proportional to its position.

The beautiful consequence: when the model compares two tokens, what survives the math is their **relative angle** — i.e., *how far apart they are*, not their absolute slots. Position becomes a geometry of relationships.

```
Token at position 1  ->  rotate by 1*theta
Token at position 50 ->  rotate by 50*theta
When they 'meet' in attention, only the GAP (49) matters.
```

## Why this powered the context explosion

Because RoPE encodes *relative* distance, clever tricks (position interpolation, NTK-aware scaling, YaRN) let engineers **stretch** a model trained on, say, 8K tokens to handle 128K or more — by gently squeezing the rotation frequencies. This 'context extension' was a major reason windows ballooned so fast without full retraining.

> RoPE is now the de facto positional scheme in modern open models. It is the quiet hero behind the long-context headlines.
slides.speakerNotes: Don't drown them in trigonometry. The takeaway: RoPE encodes RELATIVE position via rotation, and that's what lets us STRETCH context windows after training. Ask: 'Why is relative distance more useful than absolute position for understanding language?'
slides.marketConnection: Context-extension methods like YaRN became standard community practice in 2024-2026 — letting open-weight models be fine-tuned to far longer windows cheaply, narrowing the gap with closed labs.

slides.title: A Million Tokens — But Can It Actually Read Them?
slides.body:
## The headline vs. the reality

By 2026 the marketing reads like a horsepower war: 128K, 200K, 1M, 2M tokens. A million tokens is roughly **1,500 pages** — a small library in a single prompt.

But here is the uncomfortable truth every practitioner learns: **a big context window is not the same as good memory.**

## 'Needle in a Haystack' and the lost middle

Researchers run a classic test: hide one odd fact (a 'needle') somewhere in a giant document (the 'haystack') and ask the model to find it. The findings, repeated across many models:

- Models are sharp at the **beginning** and **end** of a long context.
- They often get **fuzzy in the middle** — the famous *'lost in the middle'* effect.
- Performance degrades as the window fills, even when the official limit is far higher.

So there are two numbers that matter:
- **Stated context** — what the spec sheet brags about.
- **Effective context** — how much the model can *reliably use* before quality sags.

## Why this matters for your product

Stuffing a million tokens into a prompt is:
- **Slow** — more tokens, more latency.
- **Expensive** — you pay per input token.
- **Not always smarter** — relevant retrieval (RAG, Day 12-ish thinking) often beats brute-force stuffing.

> **Rule of thumb for 2026:** Long context is a powerful tool, not a substitute for thinking about *what* you put in the window.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the slide that makes the class wiser, not just informed. Ask: 'Your vendor says 2 million token context. What test would YOU run before trusting it?' (Answer: needle-in-haystack across positions.) Stress effective vs stated context — it's a great BS-detector.
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, buyers have grown sophisticated: 'effective context' and independent long-context benchmarks now carry more weight than the headline window size that dominated 2024 launch slides.

slides.title: The Challengers: What If Attention Isn't All You Need?
slides.body:
## Heresy with a point

The Transformer's quadratic cost is structural. So a bold question emerged around 2023-2024: can we get Transformer-quality results with **linear** cost — where doubling the input only *doubles* the work?

Two big families of challengers:

### 1. State-Space Models (SSMs) — Mamba
Instead of comparing every token to every other token, an SSM maintains a **compressed running state** as it reads — like a person reading a novel who keeps an evolving mental summary rather than re-reading every prior page.

- **Cost grows linearly** with length.
- Naturally suited to *very* long sequences (audio, DNA, long documents).
- **Mamba** (Gu and Dao, 2023) added 'selectivity' — the model learns *what to remember and what to forget*, which closed much of the quality gap with attention.

```
Transformer:  every token re-examines ALL past tokens   (N x N)
SSM / Mamba:  carry a running state, update token by token (N)
```

### 2. Linear Attention
Reformulate the attention math so it avoids ever building the N x N matrix, trading some expressiveness for linear scaling. Many variants (Performer, RetNet-style ideas, and newer linear-attention hybrids) live here.

## The honest scorecard
- **Strength:** spectacular efficiency at long sequences; small, fast memory footprint.
- **Weakness:** pure SSMs can struggle with tasks needing precise, random-access recall (attention is *great* at 'look back at exactly that token').
- **The winning move:** **hybrids** — mix a few attention layers into a mostly-SSM stack to get the best of both.
slides.speakerNotes: Frame Mamba as 'a reader with a running summary' vs Transformer as 'a reader who re-reads everything.' Make clear the field hasn't crowned a winner — hybrids are the pragmatic answer. Ask: 'For a 10-hour audio file, which would you bet on?'
slides.marketConnection: By 2026, hybrid architectures (attention + state-space layers) shipped in real production models — notably in efficiency-focused and on-device offerings — proving the challengers aren't academic. Attention isn't dethroned, but it now shares the stage.

slides.title: Putting It Together: The Efficiency Stack
slides.body:
## How a modern 2026 model is built

The frontier model you call today is rarely 'just a Transformer.' It is a **stack of efficiency tricks**, each solving one of the three walls:

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  LONG CONTEXT                                    |
|   - RoPE positions (+ YaRN-style extension)      |
|   - FlashAttention (exact, hardware-aware)       |
|   - sometimes hybrid SSM/linear layers           |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  CHEAP COMPUTE                                   |
|   - Mixture-of-Experts (sparse activation)       |
|   - total params >> active params                |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  THE TRANSFORMER CORE                            |
|   - attention + feed-forward, refined since 2017 |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

## The three numbers to read on any spec sheet

1. **Active parameters** — what you *pay* per token (compute/cost).
2. **Total parameters** — what you must *host* (memory/VRAM).
3. **Effective context** — how much it can *reliably use* (not just the advertised max).

Master those three and you can decode almost any model announcement in 2026 — and call out the marketing fluff.

> The 2017 question was 'can a machine understand language?' The 2026 question is 'can we serve it to a billion people without going bankrupt?' Today's tricks are the answer.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the synthesis slide — have the class recite the three numbers back. This is the practical, durable skill from the whole day. Connect forward: Day 18 turns these into actual dollars.
slides.marketConnection: These three numbers are exactly what enterprise buyers and engineers scrutinize in June 2026 procurement decisions — the spec-sheet literacy that separates informed teams from hype-driven ones.

slides.title: Quick Check
slides.body:
## Test your instincts

**Q1.** A vendor advertises a '1 trillion parameter' model. What is the single most important follow-up question?
*(Hint: it determines your running cost.)*

**Q2.** FlashAttention makes attention faster. Does it change the model's output? Why is that surprising?

**Q3.** Your model has a 1M-token context window but performance drops on facts buried mid-document. What is this phenomenon called, and what test reveals it?

**Q4.** Mamba/SSMs scale linearly with sequence length. Name one task type where they may still lose to attention — and why teams ship *hybrids*.

**Q5.** True or false: MoE makes a model small enough to run on a laptop. Explain.

---

### Answer key
1. **How many parameters are ACTIVE per token?** Total drives memory; active drives cost.
2. **No — it's exact.** Surprising because we expect speedups to trade accuracy; this one just respects GPU memory hierarchy.
3. **'Lost in the middle';** revealed by **needle-in-a-haystack** tests across positions. Distinguish *stated* vs *effective* context.
4. **Precise random-access recall** ('quote token #4,012 exactly'). Hybrids add a few attention layers to restore that ability.
5. **False.** MoE saves compute, not memory — you must still store all experts (the memory tax).
slides.speakerNotes: Run this as a rapid-fire verbal quiz. The trillion-parameter question (Q1) is the one to drill — it's the most quotable, meeting-ready insight of the day.
slides.marketConnection: These five questions map directly onto real 2026 buying conversations — they're the literacy test for not being fooled by a launch livestream.

slides.title: Recap & Bridge
slides.body:
## What we climbed today

We started at three walls and scaled all of them:

- **The cost wall -> Mixture-of-Experts.** Huge knowledge, tiny per-token cost, via a router that wakes only the right experts. Watch *active vs total* parameters.
- **The memory wall -> long-context techniques.** FlashAttention (exact, hardware-aware) plus RoPE and context-extension tricks took us from 2K to millions of tokens — though *effective* context lags the headline.
- **The architecture wall -> the challengers.** Mamba and state-space models scale linearly; linear attention reshapes the math; hybrids quietly won the pragmatic battle.

## The one sentence to remember
> Modern AI got usable not by thinking harder, but by thinking *more efficiently* — sparser activation, smarter memory, and new blueprints that don't drown in their own length.

Tomorrow we ask a harder, more human question: even if it's big, cheap, and long — **can we trust it?**
slides.speakerNotes: Close warmly. Land the single sentence. Then tee up Day 17 with a hook: 'We just made models powerful and affordable. Now — how do we know they're telling the truth?'
slides.marketConnection: Efficiency and trust are the two axes every 2026 buyer weighs: today was efficiency, tomorrow is trust — together they define whether an AI product is shippable.

keyTakeaways: MoE = a router that activates only a few 'expert' sub-networks per token; read ACTIVE parameters for cost, TOTAL parameters for memory
MoE saves compute, not memory — it doesn't shrink the model, just its per-token running cost (the 'memory tax')
Attention's quadratic (N^2) cost was the wall; FlashAttention beat it with exact, hardware-aware tiling — same output, far cheaper
RoPE encodes relative position by rotation, enabling cheap context-extension (YaRN etc.) that ballooned windows to millions of tokens
Stated context != effective context — beware 'lost in the middle'; test with needle-in-a-haystack before trusting a big window
Mamba/SSMs and linear attention scale linearly and challenge the Transformer; in 2026 hybrids deliver the best practical trade-off
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, the efficiency frontier is the battleground. Mixture-of-Experts is the default for frontier-scale models, and 'active parameters' is a standard spec line — DeepSeek's cost-disruptive MoE releases reset market expectations on price-per-token, and the open-weight wave (Llama, Qwen, Mistral and others) made sparse, long-context models broadly accessible. FlashAttention is invisible infrastructure inside nearly every stack. Context windows reaching one to two million tokens (led publicly by the Gemini family) are now table stakes, but sophisticated buyers have learned to test EFFECTIVE context rather than trust headline numbers. Crucially, the challengers arrived: hybrid attention + state-space architectures shipped in real efficiency-focused and on-device products, proving Mamba and linear attention are production-grade, not just papers. The GPU shortage of 2025-26 made every one of these efficiency tricks a direct line item on cloud bills, turning architecture choices into board-level economics.
homework: Pick any model released or updated in the last 6 months and find its spec sheet or model card. Write down its (1) total parameters, (2) active parameters per token (or note if it's dense), (3) stated context window. Then find one independent long-context or 'needle-in-a-haystack' style result for it. In a short paragraph, argue: would you deploy this for summarizing 300-page legal contracts, and why — citing effective vs stated context and cost per token?
nextDayTeaser: We've made AI bigger, cheaper, and longer. But a confident liar is still a liar. Tomorrow — Day 17 — we confront trust: how we actually measure these models, why they hallucinate, how attackers jailbreak and inject prompts, and the alignment and regulation fights shaping who gets to control AI. The day everything gets a little more dangerous.
